# Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

# Metal-organic frameworks-derived synthesis of porous FeP nanocubes: An effective peroxidase mimetic


Wenshu Yang a,b , Jinhui Hao a,b , Zhe Zhang a , Bailin Zhang a, ⇑

a State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun130022, PR China

b University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing100049, PR China


# g r a p h i c a l a b s t r a c t


A facile process was demonstrated toward the synthesis of porous FeP nanocubes through thermal phosphidation reaction, using Prussian blue as ironbased metal-organic frameworks precursors. Then, the FeP nanocubes were exploited as a peroxidase mimetic.


# a r t i c l e i n f o


Article history: Received3May2015Revised9August2015Accepted19August2015Available online20August2015

Keywords: Metal-organic frameworks Phosphidation Porous FeP nanocubes Peroxidase mimetics

Colorimetric detection

⇑ Corresponding author. E-mail address: blzhang@ciac.ac.cn (B. Zhang).


# a b s t r a c t


Starting with metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a facile process was demonstrated toward the synthesis of porous FeP nanocubes. Herein, Prussian blue nanocubes were used as iron-based MOFs precursors, and converted into FeP nanocubes through the thermal phosphidation reaction. Then, the FeP nanocubes were exploited as a peroxidase mimetic, which not only has the advantages of low cost, high stability, and easy preparation, but also follows Michaelis-Menten behaviors and shows strong affinity to substrates. On the basis of high catalytic activity of FeP nanocubes, a rapid, and convenient approach was developed for the colorimetric detection of H2O2from2l M to130l M and with a detection limit of0.62l M. The good catalytic activity and high stability make the porous FeP nanocubes a useful catalyst for a wide range of potential applications in catalysis and biotechnology.

/C211 2015Published by Elsevier Inc.


# 1. Introduction


Peroxidases are a large family of enzymes that typically catalyze the decomposition of peroxides to product hydroxyl radicals, which can oxide many organic substrates to reduce their toxicity

[1]. With good catalytic performance, peroxidases have been applied in many fields [2-4], such as waste water treatment and adhesives in manufacturing processes [5]. Among them, horseradish peroxidase (HRP) is commonly used in techniques such as ELISA and immunohistochemistry, due to they can catalyze the conversion of chromogenic substrates (e.g., 3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethyl benzidine (TMB)) into colored products [6,7]. However, natural enzymes, being protein-based macromolecules, bear some serious drawbacks in that they are unstable against denaturation or protease digestion, and their preparation and storage are expensive [8]. To overcome the stability and cost issues of biological catalyst, lots of efforts have been made to use stable enzyme mimetics as substitutes for natural enzymes. A variety of nanomaterials, such as noble metal [9,10], carbon materials [11,12], transition metal based alternatives [13-15], have been extensively developed as enzyme mimetics. Among these materials, some iron-based metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), like PCN-222with porphyrinic Fe(III) centers [16], Fe(III)-based MIL-68 [17], MIL-100 [17] and Prussian blue [15], were recently found to be effective peroxidase mimics.

MOFs are well-ordered crystalline porous materials that consist of metal clusters coordinated to organic ligands. The crystalline structures of MOFs make them have unusual properties such as permanent nanoscale porosity, and high surface area. Thus, MOFs have been explored extensively in fields of gas storage, sensing, and catalysis [18,19]. Although the structure of MOFs is beneficial to their applications [20], unfortunately, most MOFs are unstable under alkaline conditions or ambient atmospheric environment [21,22]. Prussian blue (PB) is considered as the first synthetic MOF, which is a mixed-valence iron(III) hexacyanoferrate(II) compound. It is well known that PB would converts into Fe(OH)3in an alkaline solution [21], which will limit their practical use in various catalysis reaction processes. Transition metal phosphides are a class of well-known catalysts and have recently attracted lots of research interest in catalysis because of their excellent catalytic performance over the wide pH range [23]. On the other hand, MOFs have been demonstrated as templates/precursors to construct carbon materials or metal oxides via thermolysis [24-26], and their pores are stable during elimination of the organic ligands. Inspired by these ideas, a phosphate reaction has been conducted using PB nanocubes as precursors, and porous FeP nanocubes were obtained. Interestingly, it has been found that the as-obtained FeP nanocubes possess excellent intrinsic peroxidase-like activity and can catalytically oxidize peroxide substrate3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetrame thylbenzidine (TMB) by H2O2to produce a typical color reaction. The peroxidase-like catalytic property of FeP was further investigated through steady-state enzyme kinetics theory and methods with H2O2and TMB as the substrates. Compared with PB precursor, FeP nanocubes have better stability under alkaline conditions. Finally, the FeP were successfully used as peroxidase mimetics for the colorimetric detection of H2O2.


# 2. Experimental



# 2.1. Chemicals


3,3 0 ,5,5 0 -tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) and sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2) were obtained from Aladdin Industrial Inc. (Shanghai, China). Poly ( N -vinyl-2-pyrrolidone) (PVP, relative molecular mass30,000-40,000) and HRP was obtained from Shanghai Chemical Factory (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and potassium hexacyanoferrate(II) trihydrate (K4Fe(CN)6 /C1 3H2O) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Factory (Beijing, China). All the reagents were used as received without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water (>18.2M X cm) from a Milli-Q Plus system (Millipore).


# 2.2. Apparatus


X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained on a D8ADVANCE (Germany) using Cu K a (0.15406nm) radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed on an ESCALAB-MKII spectrometer (VG Co., United Kingdom) with Al K a X-ray radiation as the X-ray source for excitation. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were obtained with a TECNAI G2high-resolution transmission electron microscope (Holland) under an accelerating voltage of200kV, and a Hitachi H600electron microscope (Japan) under an accelerating voltage of100kV. The sample for TEM characterization was prepared by placing a drop of prepared solution on a carbon coated copper grid and drying at room temperature. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis were inspected on a Hitachi S-4800microscope. Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms were measured on an ASAP2020 (Micromeritics, USA). Pore size distribution (PSD) curves were calculated by the Barrett-JoynerHalenda (BJH) method from the absorption branch. UV-Vis detection was carried out on a PGENERAL T6UV-Vis spectrophotometer (China).


# 2.3. Synthesis of porous FeP nanocubes


To synthesize FeP nanocubes, PB nanocubes were firstly prepared according to the literature [27]. In a typical procedure, PVP (3.00g) and K3Fe(CN)6 /C1 3H2O (0.132g) were added to a HCl solution (0.1M, 40mL) under magnetic stirring. After the mixture was stirred for30min, the bottle was then placed into an electric oven and heated at80 /C176C for20h. The obtained blue product was washed several times with deionized water and finally dried at room temperature for further use. Secondly, the obtained PB nanocubes were transferred into a tubular furnace for phosphidation under N2flow. 0.05g PB and0.5g NaH2PO2powder were put at two separate positions in a fused silica tube with NaH2PO2at the upstream side of the tubular furnace. Then the PB was heated to450 /C176C at a rate of3 /C176Cmin /C0 1and maintained at this temperature for2h under protection of N2. Black FeP powder was obtained after cooled to ambient temperature under N2flow.


# 2.4. H2O2detection using porous FeP nanocubes as peroxidase mimetics


To detect H2O2, experiments were carried out using20l g FeP nanocubes in a reaction volume of2mL NaAc-HAc buffer (pH4.0) with250l M TMB as the substrate. The concentration of H2O2was20mM, unless otherwise stated. Reactions were monitored at652nm and recorded after5min at40 /C176C.


# 2.5. The steady-state kinetic assays


The steady-state kinetic assays were carried out at room temperature in a2mL of TMB-H2O2-FeP reaction system. The kinetic analysis of FeP nanocubes with TMB as the substrate was performed with TMB-H2O2-FeP system with constant H2O2concentration of5mM and10l g/mL FeP nanocubes but varied TMB concentration (50-900l M). The kinetic analysis with H2O2as the substrate was performed with constant TMB concentration of0.1mM and10l g/mL FeP nanocubes but varied H2O2concentration (1-20mM).

All the reactions were monitored in timescan mode at652nm using the UV-Vis spectrophotometer. A series of initial reaction

rates was calculated and applied to the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation, 1/ m = ( Km/ Vmax) /C1 (1/[ S ]) + 1/ Vmax, where m is the initial velocity, [ S ] is the concentration of substrate, Km is the Michaelies-Menten constant and Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity.


# 3. Results and discussion



# 3.1. Preparation and characterization of FeP nanocubes


Porous FeP nanocubes were prepared through a facile route, which involved the synthesis of PB precursors and the subsequent phosphidation reaction. The as-prepared nanocomposites were initially characterized by XRD to verify the crystal structures. Fig. 1A and B show the XRD patterns for PB and the phosphidated product. The characteristic diffraction peaks of PB precursor are in agreement with Fe4[Fe(CN)6]3 (JCPDS No.73-0687). In contrast to the sharp peaks of the original PB nanocubes, phosphidated product shows broadened and less-resolved peaks. The typical peaks of product can be identified clearly as orthorhombic FeP (JCPDS card No.78-1443) [28]. No impurity is observed, which indicates that the product is pure FeP phase.

Subsequently, the morphology of FeP nanocubes was observed by TEM. From Fig. 1C, it is noteworthy that the FeP nanocubes have a size of around150nm and possess a highly porous structure. HRTEM image (Fig. 1D) further displays that the labeled spacing of0.242nm corresponds to the (111) plane distance of FeP crystal, consistent with the XRD result. Furthermore, the samples before and after phosphidation were examined by SEM. The SEM images of PB and FeP nanocubes are shown in Fig. 2A and B. Comparing these two images, it can be found that the obtained FeP inherited the size and cubic morphology of PB precursor well, whereas their surfaces became very rough. The EDS spectrum (Fig. 2C) clearly demonstrates that both Fe and P elements exist in FeP nanocubes, which is different from the EDS results of PB, indicating the formation of FeP.

To obtain detailed information about elemental composition and chemical state of samples, XPS measurements were carried out. The survey XPS spectrum of FeP (Fig. S1) reveals the presence of P and Fe elements. High-resolution Fe2p spectrum of PB (Fig. 2D) shows two peaks at707.4and719.9eV, corresponding to the Fe2p3/2and Fe2p1/2states. However, there are two peaks (705.2and710.1eV) in the Fe2p3/2region for FeP (Fig. 2D). The peaks at128.4and129.3eV reflect the binding energy of P2p3/2and P2p1/2of FeP, respectively. The peaks at705.2and128.4eV are close to the binding energy for Fe and P in FeP [29], which indicates the formation of FeP species. The peaks at710.1and133.4eV can be assigned to oxidized Fe and P species arising from superficial oxidation of FeP exposed to air [30,31]. All these results also confirm the formation of FeP after phosphidation reaction.

Generally, the properties of nanomaterials are affected by the porous nature, morphology, and surface property. Thus, N2adsorption/desorption measurement was conducted to characterize the porous property of FeP nanocubes (Fig. 3). The isotherm curves are identified as type III, which is characteristic of larger mesoporous and macroporous materials. The BET surface area of the FeP nanocubes was calculated to be39.55m2g /C0 1 (Fig. 3). Moreover, the pore size of FeP nanocubes shows a relatively wide distribution (inset of Fig. 3). The average pore size distribution centers at /C24 12nm, implying that the sample possesses the porous structure, which is in good agreement with the result of TEM (Fig. 1C).


# 3.2. Peroxidase-like activity of FeP


The peroxidase-like activity of FeP was investigated by studying the catalytic oxidation of peroxidase substrate, TMB, in the presence of H2O2. The UV-Vis absorbance maximum of reaction mixture was found at652nm, owing to the absorption of oxidized TMB. As shown in Fig. 4A, we find that the TMB solution have no

?

Fig. 1. XRD patterns of PB (A) and FeP (B) nanocubes. TEM (C) and HRTEM (D) images of FeP nanocubes.

Fig. 2. SEM images of PB (A) and FeP (B) nanocubes. EDS analysis of PB and FeP nanocubes (C). High-resolution XPS spectra of Fe2p (D) for PB and FeP; P2p (E) spectra for FeP.

Fig. 3. N2adsorption/desorption isotherms of FeP nanocubes and the pore size distributions by the BJH method (inset).

well-developed absorption peaks in the range from400to800nm when either FeP or H2O2was present, meaning no oxidation reaction occurred. However, the TMB solution shows a strong absorption peak when both H2O2and FeP were added (Fig. 4A). The photographs (inset of Fig. 4A) for TMB solutions under different conditions have also shown the same result. All those observations indicate that FeP possesses peroxidase-like activity and can effec- tively catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2in an acidic solution [17,32].

To establish the appropriate analytical conditions, reaction conditions were optimized. The influences of working pH (Fig. 4B), reaction temperature (Fig. 4C) and the concentration of H2O2 (Fig. 4D) on catalytic activity of FeP were investigated. Meanwhile, we compared the results with the activity found in HRP over the same range of parameters (Fig. 4). When the pH and temperature are approximately4.0and40 /C176C, respectively, the FeP nanocubes show the best catalytic performance, which are very similar to the values for HRP. But the FeP nanocubes catalyzed reaction is inhibited at a higher concentration of H2O2to reach the maximum level of peroxidase activity than that of horse radish peroxidase (HRP) (Fig. 4D). In addition, the time-dependent absorbance change of TMB solution with FeP nanocubes as catalyst is higher than that of PB (Fig. S2A), which indicates that FeP nanocubes exhibit the better catalytic performance as peroxidase mimetics. To test the stability, we incubated both PB and FeP nanocubes in aqueous solution with pH values from2to14for2h (Fig. S3), and then measured the catalytic activity of FeP nanocubes under standard conditions (pH4.0and40 /C176C) (Fig. S2B). The FeP nanocubes were indeed found to remain stable over a wide range of pH from2to14 (Fig. S2B). However, from optical photographs, it can be found that the blue colors of PB nanocubes solution immediately disappeared at high pH (13and14) and became lighter at pH10, 11and12 (Fig. S4), demonstrating the instability of PB under alkaline conditions. The reusability of FeP nanocubes was also evaluated on

Inset: The optical photograph of aqueous solutions of TMB (1), TMB-H2O2 (2), TMB-FeP (3) and TMB-H2O2-FeP (4). Dependence of the peroxidase-like activity of FeP nanocubes and HRP on pH (B), temperature (C) and H2O2concentration (D).

Fig. 5. Steady-state kinetic assay and catalytic mechanism study of the FeP: the concentration of H2O2was5mM and the TMB concentration was varied (A); the concentration of TMB was0.1mM and the H2O2concentration was varied (B); the double reciprocal plots of activity of FeP with the concentration of one substrate (H2O2or TMB) fixed and the other varied (C) and (D). Error bars shown represent the standard error derived from three repeated measurements (A and B).

catalytic oxidation of TMB. After three cycles, /C24 90% of the initial catalytic activity of FeP nanocubes was retained (Fig. S4). These results show the high stability of FeP nanocubes.

To further investigate the kinetic mechanism of the peroxidaselike activity of FeP nanocubes, steady-state kinetic experiments were carried out. The velocity of the reaction at different concentrations of substrate was obtained by calculating the slopes of absorbance changes at652nm with time. In a certain range of H2O2or TMB concentration, typical Michaelis-Menten curves were observed (Fig. 5A and B). The Michaelis-Menten constant ( Km) and maximum initial velocity ( V m) were calculated from LineweaverBurk plots and listed in Table S1. It is known that Km is an indicator of enzyme affinity to substrates and a smaller Km value reflects a stronger affinity of enzyme for substrate [33]. The apparent Km

Fig. 6. Dependence of the absorbance at652nm, temperature40 /C176C and pH4.0on the concentration of H2O2 (A) and the corresponding linear calibration plots (B).

value of FeP nanocubes with TMB as the substrate is lower than those of reported Fe3O4 [34], Fe nanoparticles [35], and HRP [36], which suggests the FeP nanocubes have a higher affinity for TMB. The apparent Km value of the FeP with H2O2as the substrate is significantly higher than that for HRP (Table S1), consistent with the observation that a higher concentration of H2O2was required to observe the maximal activity for FeP nanocubes. The doublereciprocal plots (Fig. 5C and D) revealed the characteristic parallel lines of a ping-pong mechanism, as was observed for HRP [37].

On the basis of the intrinsic peroxidase property of FeP nanocubes, a colorimetric method for detection of H2O2using the FePcatalyzed blue color reaction was designed. Since the absorbance of oxidized TMB is highly dependent on H2O2concentration, this method can be used to detect H2O2. Fig. 6A shows a typical H2O2concentration-response curve under optimal conditions. The linear range of H2O2from2l M to130l M ( R2 = 0.994) was obtained, with a detection limit of0.62l M (Fig. 6B). This analytical performances show a wider linear rang and lower detection limit comparable to reported Fe3O4nanoparticles based sensor [38] (linear range: 5-100l M, detection limit: 3l M).


# 4. Conclusion


In summary, porous FeP nanocubes were successfully fabricated via a facile phosphidation method. Different from precious reported works, PB nanocubes were used as precursor to synthesize FeP nanocubes. Through simple phosphidation reaction, PB nanocubes transformed into porous FeP nanocubes with the retention of the original shapes. Moreover, the as-obtained FeP nanocubes were found exhibiting peroxidase mimetic activities. It was thoroughly observed that the peroxidase activity of the FeP nanocubes show typical Michaelis-Menten kinetics and better stability than PB in alkaline solution. On the basis of the peroxidase-like property of FeP nanocubes, a simple colorimetric method for H2O2detection was achieved. It is believed that the FeP nanocubes may be a useful artificial peroxidase for biosensing and catalysis.
